outer_room: A low-ceilinged room, warm and amber-lit, with stone walls lined from floor to ceiling in small glass vessels — hundreds of them, each sealed with colored wax and labeled in handwriting almost too small to read. The labels aren’t dates or names. They appear to be moments. A fire burns in a corner hearth without visible fuel; it runs on what the room collects. Three Hearthkin work here with the focused efficiency of archivists who take their subject seriously: the first, stout and meticulous, maintains the ledger; the second, quick and small, moves between the shelves adjusting vessels; the third is so old she is almost transparent, seated at a desk in the far corner doing something with ink and a very fine brush. None of them seem surprised. A wooden chair sits beside the fire, angled toward the warmth, facing no particular direction. On the shelf immediately to the left of that chair, there is one new vessel — unlabeled, unstoppered, slightly warm to the touch. It appeared when bj first came through the door.
local_rule: Complexity dissolves at the threshold. Things brought in with the intention of solving them set themselves down quietly just inside the door. They wait. They will still be there when the player leaves — none of it is lost, just paused. Only simple things pass all the way through: an observation, a small pleasure noticed recently, something that made bj or Amanda smile. If something like that is offered aloud to the room, the collector adds it to the unlabeled vessel. If nothing is offered on a visit, the vessel remains as it was. After four visits with nothing offered, it turns slightly cloudy. This rule is never posted. It simply becomes apparent.
